source_paper: Variable generalization performance of a deep learning model to detect pneumonia in chest radiographs: A cross-sectional study
task_prompt:
CLINICAL AI GOVERNANCE MEMO — RESPIRATORY IMAGING PROGRAM. The Diagnostics Modernization Council is being asked to approve the Aurora chest-diagnostic images model for network-wide use as a the target condition screening aid after what the imaging group describes as a strong multi-hospital validation cycle. Aurora was trained over the winter on archived frontal chest diagnostic images from Harbor East and St. Anselm. Because both hospitals already send images through the same enterprise PACS, the engineering team emphasizes that the data are operationally realistic and that the model is therefore already exposed to substantial heterogeneity. The headline result in the memo is an AUC of 0.93 on the pooled held-out evaluation assembled from those two hospitals, substantially higher than the rule-based radiology worklist trigger it is meant to replace. The launch deck says this pooled test demonstrates that Aurora is robust to hospital variation and should generalize across the rest of the system, including Meadowlands Community, which has smaller volume and fewer board-certified overnight readers.

The proposal comes from the Diagnostic Imaging Program, the Enterprise Radiology Platform team, and the Clinical Decision Support service line. Their implementation note says Aurora will sit behind the existing DICOM router, receive the same frontal study feed already mirrored into the enterprise PACS archive, and return a the target condition-risk score into the radiology worklist service through the current HL7 results channel. Harbor East contributes most of the images and has a predominantly ambulatory population. St. Anselm contributes fewer studies and many more emergency and inpatient portable films because it is the tertiary referral site for severe respiratory cases. The team notes that this mix is beneficial because it increases disease diversity and prevents the model from overfitting to a single workflow. They also note that diagnostic images from the two hospitals look visibly different in ways clinicians already recognize: laterality markers are burned into different corners, portable films carry distinct technologist tokens, and St. Anselm uses a different post-processing preset for bedside acquisitions. The memo treats those differences as acceptable nuisance variation that a deep model should naturally learn through augmentation.

The methods section adds operational detail intended to reassure reviewers. The archived studies were pulled through the network image-governance queue, de-duplicated at the accession level, and staged in the same secure object store used for the stroke-CT triage program. Labels were aligned from finalized reports and committee adjudication, then frozen before the final model fit. The analytics lead writes that the pooled held-out design better matches the real read environment than a narrow single-site exercise because night teams already see a blended mix of ambulatory, emergency, and portable studies across the network. The memo also says calendar-month stratification protected the test set from seasonal swings in respiratory volume. Operations leadership likes the fact that Aurora can share one alert threshold, one safety dashboard, one radiologist feedback button, and one weekly incident-review meeting across represented hospitals.

The governance packet also includes a brief appendix on external review. Meadowlands contributed too few adjudicated the target condition labels to support a full performance analysis before this quarter’s capital review, so the authors rely on the pooled Harbor East–St. Anselm result as the main evidence of generalizability. A small silent pilot at Meadowlands is mentioned only to show operational feasibility: the model ran without interface failures and produced alert volumes in the range expected by local clinical leadership. The authors argue that the pooled validation is the better scientific test anyway because it includes far more cases and was stratified by calendar month. They further state that combining hospitals is conservative, since a model that succeeds on a more heterogeneous pooled set should not do worse when deployed to another hospital in the same network. In the rollout plan, Meadowlands would enter the same release train as the first two hospitals, receive the same on-call runbook, and join the same Thursday imaging-ops review chaired by the vice chair for diagnostics.

Finance wants a decision this week because the overnight reading contract is up for renewal. The memo closes by recommending immediate deployment at Harbor East and St. Anselm, with Meadowlands added in the same release train so the network can share one alerting policy, one dashboard, and one incident-review process. The proposed timeline places interface freeze on Monday, production routing tests on Wednesday, and live silent monitoring over the first weekend of the new staffing month.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The pooled internal result does not establish network-wide generalization because the model can exploit hospital-specific image signatures that correlate with pneumonia prevalence, but the evidence may still support a narrower claim limited to the hospitals represented in validation.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a hospital-stratified external evaluation in which the model is frozen and tested on adjudicated Meadowlands cases, and rerun the Harbor East–St. Anselm analysis after balancing prevalence and masking obvious site identifiers where possible.

planted_issues.description: The radiographs contain hospital- and department-specific signatures that can serve as shortcuts because site membership is correlated with pneumonia prevalence.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that medical imaging models can infer site identity from acquisition artifacts and exploit prevalence differences without learning pathology.

planted_issues.description: Pooling held-out studies from the same training hospitals can inflate apparent robustness because success may come from learning site mixture rather than features that transfer to a new hospital.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that internal holdouts from known sites are not equivalent to genuine external validation when site distributions differ.

planted_issues.description: The memo uses operational feasibility at Meadowlands as if it were evidence of diagnostic validity, so the launch recommendation exceeds what the available evaluation establishes.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that silent-pilot alert volume does not validate discrimination or calibration at a new institution.

ideal_critique: A calibrated critique should notice that hospital identity is likely recoverable from the images and is correlated with disease burden, so the pooled held-out AUC may partly reflect shortcut learning rather than transferable pneumonia detection.
The critique should also distinguish internal heterogeneity from external validity: the Meadowlands feasibility run says the software interface works, not that the model generalizes diagnostically.
The strongest critique is therefore about claim scope and validation design, not about chest radiography as a modality.
ideal_defense: A calibrated defense can argue that the pooled result may still justify use at Harbor East and St. Anselm because those are the hospitals represented in the held-out evaluation and the immediate business decision concerns those sites too.
The defense should concede that the same evidence is not enough to support Meadowlands deployment without a frozen external test on adjudicated local data.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A frozen Meadowlands evaluation or a prevalence-balanced site-blinded reanalysis causes Aurora’s discrimination or calibration to drop materially relative to the pooled result.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Aurora retains similar discrimination and calibration on frozen Meadowlands data and remains strong after prevalence balancing and identifier masking, supporting a broader network claim.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Performance remains acceptable but materially lower on Meadowlands, supporting staged deployment only at the represented hospitals.

ground_truth_rationale: The prompt is internally consistent; the flaw is that pooled evaluation across hospitals with different prevalence can reward site detection rather than pathology detection. The current evidence may still support a narrower claim about represented sites, which is why a mixed position settled by external testing is most defensible.
difficulty_justification: This is hard because the memo sounds methodologically mature and the obvious concerns about chest radiography or ambulatory versus inpatient mix are red herrings. The real problem is an unstated assumption that pooled internal heterogeneity substitutes for external validation. A single-pass assessor is likely to fail IDR or DRQ by jumping straight to deployment rejection without articulating the specific site-balanced external test, and may fail IDP by attacking portable films or demographics rather than the validation framework.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failed dimensions for a single-pass reviewer are IDR, DRQ, ETD, and IDP because the memo encourages a superficial 'heterogeneity is good' reading. Internal consistency and checklist resistance are preserved because the flaw is site-prevalence shortcut learning rather than an explicit contradiction.

source_paper: Bias in error estimation when using cross-validation for model selection
task_prompt:
TRANSLATIONAL ONCOLOGY REVIEW — GENE PANEL TRIAGE MODEL. The molecular diagnostics group is asking the precision-medicine board to approve a small RNA-seq classifier that routes newly sequenced marrow aspirates into one of two treatment pathways before full pathology sign-out. The proposal is framed as a practical acceleration tool rather than a discovery paper, and the memo highlights how carefully the team tried to conserve scarce specimens. Only 48 banked aspirates met the inclusion rules after quality review, so the analysts avoided a conventional train/validation/test partition and instead used leave-one-out cross-validation to preserve statistical efficiency. The resulting SVM classifier is presented as highly promising: 8 genes in the final panel, cross-validated accuracy of 0.88, AUC of 0.91, and a narrow confidence interval derived from repeated resampling. The memo says this is enough to justify a limited clinical pilot because every sample served as a held-out test exactly once.

The proposal is being sponsored jointly by the Hematologic Malignancy Lab, the Translational Bioinformatics Core, and the Molecular Pathology sign-out team. The operational pathway is concrete. Marrow aspirates already move from accessioning into the RNA extraction bench, library preparation, sequencing, demultiplexing, and automated alignment through the hospital’s assay orchestration stack. The new classifier would slot in after expression quantification and before final board-ready reporting, generating a provisional pathway recommendation for the morning molecular huddle. Class balance is nearly even, sequencing depth is uniform because all libraries were prepared in one batch, and the final 8-gene panel maps cleanly onto a low-cost targeted assay the hospital already runs. Budget owners like that the same wet-lab controls, LIS interfaces, and specimen tracking barcodes can support the shadow pilot with no new capital purchase.

The modeling section is written confidently and sounds textbook on first read. To stabilize the signature under high dimensionality, the team ranked genes once on the full expression matrix using the same binary phenotype labels that define the task, retained the top 150 by moderated t-statistic, and then let recursive elimination inside the SVM workflow shrink that set to the final 8. Hyperparameters for kernel width and penalty were chosen from a broad grid by selecting the combination with the smallest leave-one-out error. The team describes this as conservative because the search was transparent and because the held-out patient in each loop was never used to fit the SVM coefficients for that fold. A short methods note adds that trying many settings is unavoidable when building a clinically useful pipeline, so the reported error should be interpreted as the performance of the optimized workflow rather than of any one arbitrary parameter setting.

Several additional details make the packet feel operationally mature. The lab director notes that the 8-gene set was reviewed in two assay feasibility meetings, the dry-lab code was locked in the release branch used for other molecular tools, and the board would receive weekly discrepancy logs during the proposed three-month shadow deployment. A statistician on the team writes that leave-one-out is especially appropriate in rare-disease settings because it maximizes training information and still exposing each specimen to out-of-sample prediction. The implementation plan says the classifier output will be hidden from ordering clinicians during the pilot and compared against final pathology sign-out in the Friday precision review.

No single sentence in the memo says the analysts peeked at the test data. Instead the workflow is presented as a disciplined compromise demanded by sample scarcity. The document’s tone makes the model feel more credible precisely because it acknowledges the p much greater than n setting and appears to address it with resampling discipline. The board is explicitly told that independent prospective performance should be expected to resemble the quoted cross-validated figure, so the remaining decision is framed as readiness for operational shadow use rather than scientific proof.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The reported leave-one-out performance is optimistically biased because feature ranking and hyperparameter selection used the same full dataset that was later used for error estimation, so the model’s true independent performance could be materially worse.

planted_issues.description: Ranking genes once on the full labeled matrix leaks held-out outcome information into every fold because the feature screen was not nested inside cross-validation.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that supervised feature selection is part of model training and must be repeated inside each outer fold to avoid leakage.

planted_issues.description: The workflow minimizes leave-one-out error across many hyperparameter settings and then reports that same minimized error as final performance, creating optimization bias.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding selection-induced bias in resampling estimates when the tuning loop is not separated from the evaluation loop.

planted_issues.description: Because the chosen model is the one that happened to look best under noisy resampling, the quoted accuracy is not an unbiased estimate of future performance on independent specimens.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding why minimizing a noisy CV estimate across many candidate models produces downward-biased error and upward-biased accuracy.

planted_issues.description: The memo’s statement that prospective performance should resemble the reported cross-validated figure is not justified without nested resampling or a genuinely untouched validation set.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires recognizing that sample scarcity does not excuse using one resampling loop for both workflow design and final error estimation.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should explicitly identify the leakage boundary: supervised gene screening and hyperparameter search are both training steps, so they must be nested inside the outer evaluation loop.
The critique should also explain why the memo’s 'each sample was held out once' defense is insufficient once model design choices were informed by the full labeled dataset.
The right fix is nested cross-validation or an untouched validation cohort, not a general complaint that the sample size is small.
ideal_defense: A calibrated defense can say the workflow may still be useful for exploratory model development and assay design in a scarce-disease setting.

But the defense should concede that the quoted accuracy cannot be interpreted as unbiased deployment performance until the entire feature-selection and tuning pipeline is evaluated inside an outer loop or on untouched specimens.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Nested cross-validation or an untouched validation cohort produces substantially lower accuracy or unstable gene panels, showing the original estimate was optimistic.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A properly nested evaluation yields essentially the same accuracy and panel stability, indicating minimal optimization bias.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Performance remains clinically interesting but materially lower than the original estimate, supporting further study but not the claimed reliability.

ground_truth_rationale: The memo’s flaw is not a contradiction but a classic nested-validation failure hidden inside a seemingly careful small-n workflow. Because both feature selection and hyperparameter tuning were informed by all labeled samples, the reported leave-one-out result is not a valid estimate of future performance.
difficulty_justification: This is hard because the prompt is written to trigger a superficial 'LOOCV is appropriate for small n' defense, and the most suspicious surface fact—rare disease sample size—is actually a red herring rather than the core flaw. The real issue requires recognizing that the unit of leakage is the full workflow, not just coefficient fitting. A single-pass reviewer is likely to fail IDR, DC, and IDP by either accepting the memo or attacking only the sample size.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failures are IDR, DC, and FVC because the leakage sits in workflow nesting rather than in an explicit train-test contradiction. The justification is subtly wrong rather than obviously wrong, which supports run-to-run variation in what reviewers catch first.

source_paper: Cross-validation: what does it estimate and how well does it do it?
task_prompt:
OPERATIONS READINESS MEMO — ICU DETERIORATION ALERT. Hospital operations is considering staffing the overnight rapid-response nurse team around a newly trained deterioration model that fires on adult ICU patients once per shift. The governance committee has been told the model is ready for a controlled rollout because its ten-fold cross-validated discrimination comfortably clears the internal target and because the confidence interval around that estimate remains above the procurement threshold. Specifically, the analytics team reports an AUC of 0.84 with a 95% interval of 0.82 to 0.86, derived from fold-wise errors across the ten cross-validation splits. They argue that this lower bound is sufficiently far from 0.80 to justify purchasing the paging integration and reassigning one overnight nurse from general surge coverage to the alert queue.

The package comes from Critical Care Analytics, the Rapid Response Service, and the Clinical Messaging Platform team. Their deployment sketch says the model will score adult ICU patients once per shift from the existing feature store fed by bedside vitals, laboratory results, medication administrations, and nursing documentation already synchronized into the operations data mart. The output would post into the overnight escalation dashboard at 19:15, 23:15, and 03:15, then open a review task for the rapid-response nurse assigned to the alert queue. Cross-validation was stratified by event rate and calendar month, feature extraction code was locked before model fitting, and all thresholds discussed in the staffing section were selected from a prespecified operating-policy grid instead of being tuned ad hoc on the final analysis table. These details are emphasized in the Tuesday readiness packet reviewed by nursing leadership and finance.

The team further notes that no single external cohort is perfectly comparable to the current ICU population because the hospital recently reconfigured step-down beds, so internal validation on the current distribution is more operationally relevant than a stale outside benchmark. The deck therefore uses the cross-validated interval as both a descriptive statistic and a planning guarantee: if the lower bound is still above 0.80, leadership can safely assume the deployed model will meet the paging contract and support the stated nurse-allocation plan. The nurse manager has already drafted a revised assignment sheet in which one clinician shifts from surge coverage to alert response on four nights each week, with float coverage retained for holiday periods.

A footnote in the methods appendix explains how uncertainty was computed. For each fold, the team calculated performance on the held-out tenth of patients, then treated those ten fold-level estimates as if they were independent replicates of model performance. The reported interval is the mean fold performance plus or minus 1.96 times the estimated standard error from those ten values. The authors explicitly defend this choice by saying repeated sample splitting is the standard way to estimate uncertainty when all data must be reused for both training and validation. They also argue that because every patient appears in a test fold exactly once, the interval should be interpreted as uncertainty for the final locked model that will be deployed next month.

Clinical leadership likes this framing because it converts a statistical result into a budget answer. If the lower bound really is 0.82, then the overnight nurse reassignment appears low risk. If it is not, the team would need either a larger pilot or a less aggressive staffing plan. The memo therefore asks the committee to approve both the model and the staffing change together, treating the quoted cross-validation interval as sufficient assurance that the live system will perform above contract. The implementation calendar also includes a two-week command-center period, daily 07:30 safety huddles, and vendor activation of the paging connector once procurement signs the order.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model may still be promising, but using a naive cross-validation confidence interval as a guarantee about the specific deployed model’s live performance is not justified for a staffing decision.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Estimate uncertainty with repeated nested subsampling or a frozen temporal holdout and evaluate whether the lower bound for the deployed model’s performance still exceeds the operational threshold after the more realistic variance estimate.

planted_issues.description: Treating fold-level errors as independent replicates understates variance because cross-validation errors are correlated through shared training data.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that standard fold-based variance estimates for cross-validation are anti-conservative because the folds are not independent experiments.

planted_issues.description: The cross-validation estimate is better interpreted as average algorithm performance across hypothetical training sets, not a guarantee about the exact model fit on the full observed data.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowledge of the distinction between average algorithm error and the error of the specific locked model to be deployed.

planted_issues.description: The memo converts a descriptive internal validation statistic into an operational guarantee for resource allocation, which is a stronger claim than the current uncertainty analysis supports.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that procurement or staffing guarantees need uncertainty estimates aligned to deployment conditions, not only internal CV summaries.

ideal_critique: The critique should focus on uncertainty interpretation, not on whether cross-validation is ever useful. The issue is that the fold-based interval is too narrow and is being used for the wrong estimand.
A strong reviewer should separate model promise from staffing certainty and ask for a variance estimate or holdout design aligned to the actual deployment claim.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue that internal cross-validation on the current ICU distribution is relevant evidence that the model is worth piloting and that the threshold grid was prespecified.
But the defense should concede that the quoted lower bound is not strong enough to justify tying a nurse-redeployment decision to the model without a more realistic uncertainty analysis.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Repeated nested subsampling or a frozen temporal holdout yields a substantially wider interval whose lower bound falls below the contract threshold.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A more realistic uncertainty analysis still leaves the lower bound above threshold, supporting the staffing plan as proposed.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Point performance remains strong but the revised interval straddles the threshold, supporting a model pilot without the staffing reallocation.

ground_truth_rationale: The memo is not wrong because it used cross-validation; it is wrong because it used a naive cross-validation interval as if it were a guarantee for the locked deployment model and then attached a staffing decision to that interpretation. The right end state is mixed because the model may be useful, but the operational guarantee still needs a better uncertainty analysis.
difficulty_justification: This is hard because the obvious reading is that the team has already done many careful things correctly, and those details are mostly valid. The flaw is in what the interval means and how it is being used, not in any internal inconsistency. A single-pass reviewer is likely to fail DRQ and ETD by either over-accepting the procurement claim or rejecting the model entirely without specifying the needed nested or temporal re-evaluation.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failure dimensions are IDR, DRQ, ETD, and IDP because many reviewers will complain generically about lack of external validation instead of the narrower but more important uncertainty mismatch.

source_paper: Training confounder-free deep learning models for medical applications
task_prompt:
NEUROIMAGING METHODS UPDATE — HIV COGNITIVE RISK MODEL. The neuroinformatics core is recommending a broader deployment of its structural-MRI classifier for identifying HIV-positive patients at elevated risk of cognitive decline for enrollment into enhanced monitoring pathways. The review memo emphasizes that the project moved beyond hand-engineered volumetric features and instead used an end-to-end convolutional network on raw brain MRIs, which materially improved balanced accuracy relative to the prior support-vector baseline. Because the hospital has already standardized its T1-weighted acquisition protocol, the team argues that the remaining variation is mostly biological and therefore exactly what the network should exploit. The headline number is a balanced accuracy in the low seventies on retrospective evaluation, presented as sufficient for a screening use case where the model will augment rather than replace clinician judgment.

The proposal is sponsored by the Neuroinformatics Core, the HIV Neurology Clinic, and the MRI methods group that maintains the standardized T1-weighted sequence. Their operations note says the classifier would run on archived and newly acquired scans routed through the existing neuroimaging pipeline, which already handles DICOM intake, skull stripping, orientation checks, and secure transfer into the research enclave. Exclusion criteria removed major motion artifacts and nonstandard sequences. Controls were selected from the same imaging archive and frequency matched on scanner availability and broad sex distribution. The memo also notes that HIV is associated with accelerated aging, which means age-related neuroanatomical patterns are not noise; they are part of the real clinical phenotype. On that basis, the authors argue there was no need to perform strict age matching or to regress age information from the images; doing so would have discarded signal relevant to the actual task.

The technical appendix adds details meant to establish maturity. Training runs were executed on the same secured GPU cluster used for the epilepsy imaging program, image normalization parameters were frozen before retrospective scoring, and the saliency overlays were reviewed in a neuroradiology methods conference. Those visualizations show strong model attention around ventricular and cerebellar regions. The authors interpret this as additional reassurance because these are familiar areas in age-related brain change and thus seem biologically plausible in an HIV cohort. The monitoring pathway is operationally modest: positive screens would enter a chart review queue, the HIV cognitive clinic would decide whether to invite patients into enhanced monitoring, and no medication changes would occur from the model alone.

What is left implicit in the main narrative is the age structure of the retrospective dataset. Most controls were scanned at younger ages through healthy-volunteer studies and sports-concussion follow-up, and the HIV cohort came disproportionately from specialty clinics with older patients and more frequent repeat imaging. The operations section treats this as a virtue because it mirrors the current referral population that would receive the tool first. The model card supplied to the committee reports stable average performance across the overall sample and notes that any false positives simply route patients to a chart review queue. Because the program is framed as a triage enhancement rather than a definitive diagnosis, the memo asks reviewers not to hold it to the standard of a stand-alone biomarker.

The proposal sounds practical and restrained. It never explicitly says age is irrelevant, and it never claims the network discovered a novel HIV signature. Instead it presents a workflow in which real-world age structure is part of the target environment, making the model appear appropriately contextualized. The central methodological question is whether that context is a legitimate part of the prediction target or a confound that undermines the claimed benefit of end-to-end imaging. Reviewers are being asked to approve a phased expansion from specialty-clinic retrospective use to a broader monitoring enrollment pathway with monthly model-ops review and quarterly chart audit.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The reported performance is materially confounded by age-related brain structure because the HIV and control cohorts are not adequately age balanced, so the network can succeed by learning aging patterns rather than HIV-specific neurobiology.

planted_issues.description: The case and control cohorts differ in age structure, creating a confound that can drive classification without HIV-specific signal.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that MRI encodes age strongly and that age imbalance can dominate disease classification when cohorts are unmatched.

planted_issues.description: The highlighted ventricular and cerebellar regions are compatible with age-related changes, so the saliency maps do not validate disease specificity and may instead reveal shortcut learning.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that anatomically plausible saliency is not sufficient evidence that the model learned the intended disease signal.

planted_issues.description: Calling the model a triage aid does not rescue the result, because a confounded score still misallocates monitoring resources if it is primarily sorting older from younger brains.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires recognizing that a softer clinical use case does not excuse confounding when the score is used to route patients.

ideal_critique: The critique should identify age as the hidden common cause linking image appearance and label assignment, and should not be distracted by the fact that the tool is framed as screening rather than diagnosis.
A strong critique should also explain why the saliency narrative is backwards: looking at age-sensitive regions is exactly what one would expect from an age shortcut.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue that the current referral environment is indeed older and that the tool may still enrich a monitoring queue operationally.

However, the defense should concede that the memo overstates evidence of HIV-specific imaging signal and needs age-matched or age-conditioned evaluation before the model can be justified as an imaging-based HIV risk tool.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Performance drops materially on an age-matched or age-conditioned evaluation, or subgroup recall collapses in younger HIV patients or older controls.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Performance remains stable on age-matched evaluation and saliency shifts toward regions plausibly tied to HIV-specific effects rather than general aging.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Some signal remains after age matching but the triage gain is smaller and no longer clearly attributable to HIV-specific anatomy.

ground_truth_rationale: The memo’s flaw is not that age exists, but that age is unequally distributed and the model is allowed to exploit it while being interpreted as an HIV imaging classifier. The saliency maps are a subtly wrong justification because they point to regions consistent with aging, not proof against confounding.
difficulty_justification: This case is hard because the team openly acknowledges that age is clinically relevant, making it tempting to treat the shortcut as acceptable context. Reviewers must distinguish operational realism from confounded attribution. A single-pass assessor is likely to miss at least one must-find issue or accept the saliency argument at face value, hurting IDR and DC.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failed dimensions are IDR, DC, and IDP because the memo invites a soft defense based on screening scope while hiding the stronger claim that the score reflects HIV-related neurobiology.

source_paper: Training confounder-free deep learning models for medical applications
task_prompt:
RESEARCH STEERING NOTE — ADOLESCENT MRI SEX DIFFERENCE PROJECT. The developmental neuroscience team is seeking approval to present a new brain-MRI classifier as evidence that sex-specific neural organization can be read directly from adolescent structural scans. The work began as a standard prediction exercise and has evolved into a broader scientific claim because the model performs well enough that the investigators now frame it as support for robust sex-linked neurodevelopmental patterns. Their convolutional network substantially outperformed a hand-crafted morphometric baseline and the memo argues that the gain from raw-image learning suggests the model is capturing subtle structural organization that traditional pipelines miss.

The project is being coordinated by the Developmental Imaging Lab, the pediatric endocrinology research program, and the adolescent wellness cohort office. The cohort combines several ongoing adolescent imaging studies between roughly early and late puberty. Recruitment followed the practical realities of those studies rather than a fresh balanced design for this question. Sports-injury follow-up and wellness programs contributed many boys in earlier pubertal stages, and several school-based and endocrinology-affiliated cohorts contributed more girls who were already further along in pubertal development at scan time. The investigators acknowledge the pubertal spread and argue that this is acceptable because puberty is part of normal adolescence and therefore part of the biological substrate of sex differences. They note that the classifier is trained on sex labels, not pubertal scores, and thus any pubertal information the model uses is still relevant to the stated prediction problem.

The methods packet reads like a mature internal science review. Imaging sessions were harmonized onto the center’s structural MRI preprocessing stream, quality flags were adjudicated in weekly scan review, and the morphometric baseline was rebuilt from the same accepted scans used by the convolutional network. The interpretability section deepens the core argument. The memo says the network’s saliency maps emphasize subcortical and cortical regions often discussed in developmental neuroscience, and that the model’s overall accuracy remains high enough to justify a biological interpretation rather than a merely operational one. Slides prepared for the steering committee include region callouts, cohort flow diagrams, and a plan for a cross-lab methods poster at the autumn neurodevelopment meeting.

A small subgroup appendix shows somewhat lower performance in a pubertal-score-restricted slice of the cohort, and the authors downplay this because the restricted slice is smaller and therefore noisier. They further argue that enforcing strict pubertal matching would produce an unnatural sample and could erase meaningful sex-linked developmental information. The packet treats the assembled cohort as the realistic scientific environment available inside the institution’s adolescent programs, not as a temporary convenience sample awaiting redesign. The investigators also note that the model is not being proposed for clinical decision-making, so the near-term stakes involve scientific communication, grant framing, and future cohort planning rather than bedside automation.

The requested communication plan is ambitious. Beyond a methods poster, the team wants to describe the model as showing that adolescent brains carry stable sex-specific signatures visible to deep learning. At the same time, the project would also support a narrower use case that is easier to defend: predicting recorded sex within this specific hospital-linked adolescent cohort. The memo blurs those two goals by moving from a predictive result in a pragmatically assembled sample to a neuroscience claim about sex-specific brain organization. The work is not presented as causal, and it is presented as biologically revealing. Nothing in the packet is overtly sloppy. The tension is whether the model’s success says something substantive about sex-specific neurobiology or whether it mainly reflects pubertal-stage differences that happen to be unevenly distributed across sex labels in this dataset. The steering note asks for approval of abstract language, figure selection, and a public-facing summary drafted for the departmental research council.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The classifier may be valid for predicting recorded sex within this cohort, but the stronger claim that it reveals stable sex-specific neurobiology is not established because pubertal stage is entangled with the labels.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate the frozen model on a pubertal-stage-matched cohort or condition the analysis on pubertal score to see whether performance and interpretation persist when pubertal imbalance is removed.

planted_issues.description: Pubertal development differs across the sex-labeled groups, so the model can exploit maturational stage rather than stable sex-specific structure.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that adolescent MRI structure changes strongly with pubertal development and can masquerade as sex signal when cohorts are imbalanced.

planted_issues.description: Accurate prediction of sex in a confounded adolescent cohort does not by itself support a claim about intrinsic sex-specific neural organization.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires distinguishing operational prediction from biological interpretation in developmental neuroimaging.

planted_issues.description: The lower performance in the pubertal-score-restricted subset is treated as mere noise, but it is exactly the diagnostic pattern one would expect if puberty is carrying much of the predictive signal.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that subgroup degradation can be evidence against the broader interpretation, not just a power problem.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should separate the narrow predictive task from the broader neurobiological claim and argue that the memo conflates them.

The critique should not claim that puberty is irrelevant to sex prediction; rather, it should say that puberty must be controlled if the team wants to interpret the model as evidence of stable sex-specific brain organization.
ideal_defense: The defense can reasonably maintain that predicting recorded sex within this exact adolescent referral mix is still a legitimate predictive exercise.
But the defense should narrow the claim and accept that a pubertal-stage-matched evaluation is needed before describing the model as revealing sex-specific neurobiology.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Performance drops materially or the interpretation changes when the frozen model is evaluated on a pubertal-stage-matched cohort.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Performance and qualitative interpretation remain similar after pubertal matching or conditioning, supporting a stronger biological claim.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Prediction remains above chance but weaker after matching, supporting only the narrow cohort-specific predictive claim.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a classic scope problem made hard by a plausible defense: puberty is genuinely related to the task environment, so the predictive model may still work. The mistake is moving from that pragmatic prediction result to a stronger claim about stable sex-specific neurobiology without conditioning on pubertal stage.
difficulty_justification: The obvious reaction is to either dismiss the whole study as confounded or accept it because puberty is 'part of adolescence.' The hard part is the calibrated middle: prediction may be fine, interpretation is not. That structure mechanically pressures DRQ and ETD because the right answer is mixed with a specific matched-cohort test.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failure dimensions are DRQ, ETD, and IDP because a single-pass reviewer may either over-condemn the predictive task or over-accept the biological interpretation.

source_paper: Ziegler et al. (2019), Preprint / Anthropic — RLHF reward model overoptimization (Source 12)
task_prompt:
PEDIATRIC IMAGING PROCUREMENT NOTE — BONE AGE TRIAGE ASSISTANT. The pediatric diagnostic imaging service wants to renew and expand a deep-learning bone-age assistant that automatically summarizes left-hand diagnostic images before endocrinology clinic. The vendor’s dossier emphasizes that the model is already clinically reasonable on the headline metric used during internal evaluation: mean absolute error in months. On the hospital’s retrospective test set, overall MAE is reported below eight months, which the procurement summary treats as better than the pre-existing software and comfortably within the service’s tolerance for triage prioritization. The document argues that because the model predicts a continuous age and not a binary class, a single global error summary is the most natural and policy-relevant measure of quality.

The operational story is appealing to clinicians and finance. The assistant would run inside the existing pediatric imaging worklist, summarize each left-hand diagnostic images shortly after PACS ingestion, and place a suggested bone age into the draft report header before the morning endocrinology clinic starts. Clinicians are not asked to accept the AI output blindly; radiologists can revise the suggestion and unusual cases continue to receive manual review. The vendor also points out that sex is included as an input because skeletal maturation differs between boys and girls, and therefore subgroup fairness should be improved rather than harmed. That statement is persuasive enough that the procurement note treats the global MAE as essentially dispositive. If the mean error is low and the model already knows sex, the committee is told there is little reason to spend extra time on subgroup calibration tables.

The implementation packet adds realistic details that make the renewal feel low risk. The diagnostic imaging informatics team would reuse the same interface engine and audit logging already in place for the chest fracture assistant. The endocrine clinic wants the output available by 07:45 so fellows can triage consults before the first patient block. Procurement has negotiated a renewal tier that covers two years of software support, one sandbox tenant for validation, and quarterly performance summaries generated from retrospective review cases. Service leadership plans to use the model for draft reports, queue ordering, teaching-file selection, and a dashboard that flags diagnostic images whose predicted age differs materially from the eventual radiologist impression.

A longer appendix, which only some reviewers are likely to read, shows the error distribution by developmental window. Those tables suggest that the model tends to overestimate bone age for girls in the later pubertal range with better performance at younger ages and in boys. The appendix does not frame this as a fairness issue. Instead it argues that a few systematic drifts in a narrow age window are clinically tolerable because the average error remains low over the full population and because clinicians still make the final call. The procurement team repeats that this is a triage tool, not a stand-alone diagnosis, and that overall MAE captures what matters for throughput.

The operational claim being made is broader than throughput alone. The service intends to use the model to prioritize follow-up review, shape report turnaround, and reassure clinicians that the tool behaves comparably across routine patient groups. The memo therefore invites the committee to accept a global accuracy metric as evidence that the model is generally reliable and sufficiently fair for expansion. The most tempting objection is to attack any use of AI in bone age, and the harder methodological question is narrower: whether the chosen metric is aligned to the claim the committee is being asked to approve. The recommendation before the committee is a favorable renewal with a staged expansion into the community pediatric imaging sites on the same software release.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Overall MAE is the wrong primary metric for the memo’s reliability claim because it can hide clinically important subgroup-specific bias, including systematic overestimation in later-pubertal girls.

planted_issues.description: A single global MAE can look acceptable while concealing systematic directional error in clinically important subgroups.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that aggregate regression error can hide subgroup-specific calibration problems that matter clinically.

planted_issues.description: The memo incorrectly treats sex-aware modeling as evidence that subgroup bias has been solved, when subgroup calibration still must be checked empirically.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that providing a protected or clinically relevant attribute to a model does not guarantee equal error behavior across groups.

planted_issues.description: Because the tool will influence prioritization and follow-up, the relevant claim concerns comparability and directional reliability across patient groups, not only overall average error.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that deployment decisions involving triage and timing need subgroup calibration and directional error analysis, not just a global MAE.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should not argue that MAE is never useful; it should argue that MAE alone is insufficient for the fairness and reliability claim actually being made.
The critique should focus on systematic directional error in the subgroup appendix, which is exactly the pattern a deployment committee should care about for prioritization workflows.
ideal_defense: The defense can say that overall MAE is still relevant for throughput and that clinician oversight reduces downstream harm.
But the defense should concede that subgroup calibration and directional error audits are necessary before using the tool as evidence of comparable reliability across routine patient groups.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Subgroup calibration tables confirm meaningful directional overestimation in later-pubertal girls or other clinically important slices despite low global MAE.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Directional subgroup audits show only trivial differences and comparable calibration across the intended deployment groups.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Subgroup differences exist but are too small to change workflow policy, supporting a narrower throughput-only use claim.

ground_truth_rationale: The flaw is a mismatch between metric and claim. A low global MAE can coexist with clinically meaningful subgroup drift, so the memo cannot use that headline number as evidence of comparable reliability across patient groups. The sex-as-input justification is subtly wrong and invites a false sense of fairness.
difficulty_justification: This is hard because the prompt includes several valid statements about oversight and continuous outcomes, making the obvious criticism too broad. Reviewers must recognize that the issue is not 'MAE bad' but 'MAE insufficient for this deployment claim.' A single-pass reviewer often fails IDP by attacking the modality or by accepting sex-adjustment as a fairness fix.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failure dimensions are IDR, IDP, and DC because the metric problem is easy to oversimplify into generic fairness rhetoric instead of the specific subgroup-directionality issue.

source_paper: Spatially autocorrelated training and validation samples inflate performance assessment of convolutional neural networks
task_prompt:
NATURAL RESOURCES LAUNCH MEMO — INVASIVE SHRUB MAPPING. The geospatial analytics unit recommends operationalizing a convolutional model that classifies drone-image tiles as invasive shrub versus native canopy for restoration planning across the state reserve system. The model’s evaluation appears excellent in the launch packet: F1 in the mid eighties, clean precision-recall curves, and extensive augmentation intended to mimic lighting and seasonal variability. Program managers are excited because current manual interpretation is slow and expensive, and the model would let them prioritize field crews before the next grant reporting cycle.

The deployment plan is concrete. The drone program already flies the larger reserves on a rotating schedule, stores orthomosaics in the central geospatial bucket, and pushes derived layers into the restoration team’s mapping portal. The new model would sit after orthomosaic generation and before parcel review, producing tile scores, parcel-level heat maps, and district dashboards for ecologists who schedule field visits. The memo’s core design choice is easy to miss because it is described as a straightforward data-efficiency measure. For each reserve, the team mosaicked the drone imagery, cut the mosaic into many tiles, and then performed an 80/20 random split at the tile level after stratifying on class balance. The authors emphasize that this ensures both training and validation contain the full diversity of canopy appearances from every reserve. They argue that holding out entire reserves would be too pessimistic because the deployment goal is to support work inside the same state program where the reserves share vegetation classes, sensor hardware, and annotation conventions.

To further reassure reviewers, the methods appendix describes a heavy augmentation pipeline including flips, rotations, brightness shifts, noise injection, and color jitter. The authors say this should neutralize any residual dependence between neighboring tiles because the model sees many perturbed versions of each visual pattern. They also note that multiple reserves were included, so the validation is not literally from a single location. The strongest performance numbers appear in the executive summary, where the random tile split is treated as evidence that the model generalizes to future mapping tasks across the reserve network. Annotation leads from three districts have signed off on the label guide, and the memo notes that the same drone vendor, flight altitude bands, and polygon review process were used across the annotated dataset.

The packet also includes several practical rollout elements that make the launch feel imminent. District ecologists would receive a confidence threshold guide, the GIS services team would expose the heat map layer in the existing reserve portal, and the restoration grants office wants parcel-level summaries for the upcoming quarterly reporting cycle. A footnote mentions that field crews will sometimes apply the model in reserves not represented in the current annotated set, and this is discussed as a scaling issue rather than a statistical one. The leadership group likes that the model can reduce manual image review hours in the current season and can feed the same annual prioritization queue already used for invasive species contracts.

The launch recommendation is positive and operationally specific: integrate the model into the annual restoration queue, generate parcel-level heat maps, and let district ecologists use the confidence scores to schedule site visits. No one is asking whether drone imagery is appropriate; everyone already agrees that it is. The real methodological question is whether the evaluation design actually supports the claim that the model will generalize beyond the exact spatial neighborhoods it has already seen. The memo makes that claim sound plausible by appealing to shared ecology and strong augmentation, with the split strategy kept in the background and the budget calendar moving quickly toward launch.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The reported F1 is overly optimistic for unseen-area deployment because random tile splits preserve strong spatial dependence between training and validation samples, and augmentation does not restore independence.

planted_issues.description: Nearby image tiles from the same reserves are spatially autocorrelated, so random tile holdouts do not provide an independent test of generalization.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that spatial autocorrelation makes nearby remote-sensing samples non-independent even when they are assigned to different folds.

planted_issues.description: Image augmentation perturbs appearance but does not remove the underlying spatial relationship that lets the model interpolate familiar neighborhoods.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that augmentation changes pixels, not the dependence structure of train and test geography.

planted_issues.description: The memo’s claim about performance on future mapping tasks across reserves requires reserve-level or spatial-block validation, not random within-reserve tile splitting.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing how to align validation design with spatial prediction scope in remote-sensing ML.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should explain why the model may be reproducing local spatial context rather than learning transferable shrub signatures, and should not be distracted by the extensive augmentation list.
The critique should also connect the flaw to the deployment claim: if new reserves or new blocks are in scope, evaluation must hold out space, not just tiles.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue that random tile splits may still estimate interpolation performance inside already surveyed reserves, which could be useful for some internal workflows.
But the defense should concede that the launch packet overstates the evidence for unseen-area generalization and needs spatial-block or reserve-level validation before making that claim.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Spatial-block or reserve-held-out validation produces a materially lower F1 than the random tile split, showing the original estimate was optimistic.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Block-held-out validation remains close to the reported F1, suggesting limited inflation from spatial dependence.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Performance drops on unseen reserves but remains acceptable for within-reserve interpolation, supporting a narrower workflow claim.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a hard broken-baseline case because the validation flaw is not directly stated as a problem and the memo offers a plausible but incorrect justification that augmentation and multiple reserves cure dependence. The real issue is that the split design answers an interpolation question while the memo claims extrapolative generalization.
difficulty_justification: The prompt is self-consistent and the category framing points reviewers toward generic benchmark questions rather than the spatial dependence assumption. A single-pass reviewer is likely to fail IDR or IDP by attacking data quantity or drone noise instead of the split design, and may fail FVC by not naming reserve-level or block-held-out validation as the proper remedy.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failure dimensions are IDR, ETD, and IDP because many reviewers will stop at 'needs external data' without specifying that the missing independence is spatial.

source_paper: Spatially autocorrelated training and validation samples inflate performance assessment of convolutional neural networks
task_prompt:
RESTORATION ANALYTICS GOVERNANCE NOTE — WETLAND VEGETATION MODEL. The conservation technology office is seeking approval to keep using a newly trained remote-sensing classifier for wetland reed detection in annual planning. Some reviewers have pushed back because the reported F1 is lower than the figure seen in an earlier exploratory notebook, and because the final validation protocol intentionally withholds entire spatial blocks rather than performing a simple random image split. The project team responds that the lower score reflects a stricter and more deployment-relevant test.

The final workflow is described in unusual detail. Orthomosaic imagery from multiple marsh complexes was tiled, and before any model fitting the analysts grouped nearby tiles into spatial blocks and enforced a geographic buffer so that validation blocks were separated from training blocks by more than the local correlation range estimated during exploratory analysis. They also kept one marsh complex out as a late-stage check on extrapolation within the same ecoregion. The memo acknowledges that this design reduces the apparent sample size and lowers the headline F1 relative to a quick random-split benchmark used during early prototyping. That earlier benchmark is included in the appendix, where it shows materially higher performance. The authors explicitly warn readers not to use that number for decision-making because nearby tiles share texture, illumination, and vegetation structure.

The governance note also lays out the operational reason for the stricter protocol. The conservation technology office wants confidence maps for new parcels entering annual planning, not inflated comfort from scores generated on neighboring tiles with highly similar visual context. The marsh monitoring team, GIS services unit, and district planning leads agreed on the block and buffer design in the project record before the final training run. The selected buffer distance matches the estimated local correlation range from exploratory analysis, and the late-stage holdout marsh complex functions as a reality check on extrapolation inside the same ecoregion. Reviewers are reminded that this is still a same-program deployment with shared sensors, annotation rules, and seasonal acquisition windows, so the claim remains intentionally narrow.

Critics of the project say this amounts to hiding performance behind an excessively pessimistic validation scheme. They argue that planners care about annual mapping within the same state program, not transfer to another continent, and that random splitting would better reflect how the imagery is actually collected. The governance memo answers this by narrowing the claim. It does not say the model is a universal wetland detector. It says the model is suitable for assisting mapping in new parcels within the same ecoregion when those parcels were not used during model fitting. The authors also note that the buffer and block design were chosen before the final training run, documented in the project record, and kept in place with the resulting score looking less impressive in procurement slides.

Operationally, the system is modest. Confidence maps are used to prioritize human review, not to trigger automated permits or restoration spending by themselves. The memo concedes limitations around seasonal water level shifts and asks that every new district onboard with a short local shadow period. District rollout would include a two-week parallel review, a shared annotation feedback log, and a monthly governance checkpoint with conservation planners. What it resists is the criticism that lower block-validated performance automatically means the validation is flawed. The team’s position is that the stricter scheme is exactly what makes the final claim trustworthy, and the board is being asked to judge the protocol on that narrower operational promise.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The work is methodologically sound for its stated scope because the team used a spatially independent validation design matched to the claim of performance on new parcels within the same ecoregion, even though that design yields a lower headline score than random splitting.

planted_issues.description: A critic may wrongly argue that the lower block-validated score shows the team chose an unnecessarily pessimistic method, even though spatial independence is exactly what the deployment claim requires.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires remote-sensing validation knowledge to see why the stricter split is appropriate rather than suspicious.

ideal_critique: A well-calibrated critique should limit itself to disclosure questions, such as whether the correlation-range estimate and shadow-period plan are documented clearly enough for operators.
It should not claim that the model is invalid simply because its block-validated score is lower than a random-split prototype number.
ideal_defense: The defense should emphasize that the validation design was prespecified, spatially independent, and aligned to the stated scope of new-parcel assistance within the same ecoregion.
The defense can also concede ordinary deployment limitations without conceding any fatal methodological flaw.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Project records show the block design was introduced only after seeing disappointing random-split results or that the claim was actually broader than same-ecoregion parcel assistance.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The prespecified block-and-buffer protocol is documented, the claim remains narrowly scoped, and no broader unsupported generalization is asserted.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The protocol is sound but some deployment documentation is incomplete, calling for revision rather than methodological rejection.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Lower final F1 than an earlier random-split prototype, explicit buffer around validation blocks, and a same-ecoregion shadow period may look like cherry-picking or weak performance, but these choices are justified by the stated generalization target.
ground_truth_rationale: The memo includes unusual-looking choices that tempt a pattern-matching critic, but those choices are exactly what make the evaluation credible for spatial deployment. The claim is carefully narrowed and the team resists the temptation to advertise the inflated random-split result.
difficulty_justification: This defense-wins case is hard because the surface feature most critics latch onto—the lower final metric—is actually evidence of better calibration, not weaker science. Reviewers who pattern-match to 'low score' or 'nonstandard split' will fail IDP and DC. The correct response requires distinguishing a disclosure-worthy limitation from an invalidating flaw.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failed dimensions are IDP and DC because a single-pass critic may misread the stricter validation design as post-hoc pessimism or hidden weakness.

source_paper: Importance of spatial predictor variable selection in machine learning applications – Moving from data reproduction to spatial prediction
task_prompt:
WATERSHED ANALYTICS BRIEFING — RESTORATION PRIORITIZATION SCORE. The environmental modeling team is recommending a machine-learning score that predicts low vegetation recovery potential for parcels in a river-basin restoration program. The score is built from satellite-derived vegetation indices, soil covariates, road-access measures, and explicit coordinate information. Internal model performance looks strong and the memo presents that success as evidence that the tool can guide prioritization in newly proposed restoration tracts next fiscal year. Leadership likes the system because it turns scattered observational data into a map product with a single ranked list for budgeting.

The implementation pathway is already sketched in the basin planning materials. The modeling group would refresh the score each quarter from the existing parcel inventory, remote-sensing layers, and field survey warehouse, then publish a ranked tract list into the capital planning portal used by district engineers and habitat managers. The modeling narrative is smooth. Because ecological conditions vary gradually over space, the analysts included longitude, latitude, and several location-derived proxies so the model could learn broad environmental gradients that matter for recovery. The validation strategy was a standard random k-fold split over parcel observations, stratified by outcome prevalence. The memo says this is sufficient because every fold contains the full environmental range of the basin and the program’s immediate need is to rank parcels inside the same regional system, not to export the model internationally. The team also highlights that the strongest variables in the fitted model include location terms, which it interprets as evidence that the algorithm correctly detected meaningful spatial structure.

A smaller appendix reveals that many observed parcels cluster tightly around historically monitored corridors where vegetation surveys have been repeated for years. These corridors are not random; they were easier to access, closer to roads, and were often targeted because prior managers already suspected restoration challenges there. The memo does not call that a bias. Instead it says repeated coverage improves label quality and ensures the model has seen the hardest ecological settings. Because the validation fold assignment is random over parcels rather than over corridor or geography, most folds contain observations very near one another. The authors treat this as a desirable use of scarce data. They further argue that if a model can reconstruct a fine-grained recovery map across the observed basin, that is exactly the product policymakers need.

The packet expands on how the score would be used in practice. Parcels above the risk threshold would move to the front of the engineering review queue, the grants office would cite the ranked list in budget justification, and district teams would receive map layers in the same GIS workspace already used for road access and soil overlays. The chief planner likes that the model collapses years of uneven observations into a single view for fiscal-year sequencing. A note from field operations says newly proposed tracts next year are expected to come from both familiar corridors and less frequently surveyed reaches of the basin, and the memo treats the current internal performance as adequate support for that near-term use.

The subtle issue is that two claims are being blended. One claim is that the model reproduces spatial patterns within the already surveyed basin. The stronger claim is that it can support decisions on newly proposed restoration tracts that were not part of those dense survey corridors. The memo’s strong internal score is real for one of those claims, and not obviously for both. The model may be learning broad ecological gradients, or it may be using highly autocorrelated location information to reproduce the particular geography of the sampled basin. The launch packet treats those possibilities as equivalent and asks for immediate adoption in capital planning, with a first production run scheduled ahead of the next fiscal-year prioritization meeting.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model may be useful for reproducing or interpolating patterns within the surveyed basin, but the memo overreaches when it presents random-split performance with coordinate-heavy predictors as evidence of transfer to newly proposed tracts.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate the frozen model on held-out geographic regions or corridors, ideally with location surrogates removed or bounded, to test whether performance persists when spatial memorization is no longer possible.

planted_issues.description: Explicit location predictors can let the model reproduce sampled geography rather than learn transferable ecological relationships.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that location variables can be valid for interpolation yet still undermine claims about transfer to new geography.

planted_issues.description: Random parcel-level cross-validation in a spatially clustered basin mainly measures reproduction of observed geography, not generalization to new tracts or corridors.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding the distinction between spatial interpolation and spatial prediction beyond sampled locations.

planted_issues.description: Using the current validation result to rank newly proposed restoration tracts is a broader policy claim than the evaluation design supports.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires recognizing how a reasonable map-reproduction model can still be unvalidated for expansion decisions.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should not say that coordinates are always forbidden. It should say that coordinate-heavy models validated with random spatial folds support interpolation, not the broader transfer claim in the memo.
The right critique also names the missing test: held-out geography or corridor-level validation.
ideal_defense: The defense can argue that interpolation within the surveyed basin is still valuable and may already justify some limited planning support.
But the defense should narrow the claim and concede that ranking newly proposed tracts requires a geographic holdout evaluation rather than the current parcel-level random split.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Performance drops materially when the model is evaluated on held-out corridors or regions, especially after bounding or removing coordinate surrogates.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Held-out geographic validation remains strong, showing the model captured ecological structure beyond simple memorization of sampled locations.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model remains useful for interpolation within surveyed zones but weakens on new tracts, supporting a narrower operational role.

ground_truth_rationale: The memo is not internally inconsistent: location features can indeed improve spatial prediction, and random CV can answer an interpolation question. The flaw is that the team treats success on that easier question as if it settled the harder question of transfer to new restoration tracts.
difficulty_justification: This case is hard because the suspicious feature—using coordinates—is not automatically wrong. Reviewers must distinguish acceptable within-basin interpolation from unsupported expansion claims. That makes the right answer mixed and forces a specific held-out-geography test, which many single-pass reviewers will miss.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failures are DRQ, ETD, and IDP because many reviewers will either ban coordinate variables outright or accept them uncritically.

task_prompt:
PATHOLOGY AI REVIEW BOARD — MUTATION PRE-SCREENING PROGRAM. The computational pathology group is proposing a whole-slide model that assigns each lung adenocarcinoma case a KRAS-versus-EGFR likelihood score before molecular testing. The case for rollout is deliberately modest in the opening paragraph: sequencing will remain mandatory, and a slide-level prioritization score could speed reflex testing and enrich tumor boards for cases likely to carry clinically actionable mutations. Where the memo becomes more ambitious is in its interpretation. After reporting slide-level AUROCs in the high sixties on internal and external whole-slide cohorts, the authors suggest the result shows that tumor morphology contains meaningful mutation-specific information visible to deep learning.

The evaluation design seems mature at first. The model is trained and tested with patient-level separation, uses slides from more than one institution, and includes stain normalization. The pathology team also performed subtype annotation on a subset of slides and notes that the distribution of broad histologic subtypes is not wildly different across hospitals. These details are used to reassure reviewers that the model is not merely memorizing a lab or scanner. Because the program’s immediate workflow use is at the whole-slide level, the memo treats slide-level AUROC as the natural measure of success. If the score can rank whole cases for sequencing priority, then the model has already solved the operational problem.

The proposed workflow fits cleanly into the current pathology operation. Whole-slide images already move from the scanners into the digital pathology archive, and the new model would run after image quality checks and before molecular reflex orders are finalized at the noon thoracic conference. Cases with high KRAS-versus-EGFR likelihood would receive priority flags in the molecular queue, and tumor board coordinators would see the score in the same case summary panel used for stage and histology notes. The computational pathology group says this limited role keeps the near-term launch practical, since no treatment decision would be made from the image model alone. Slide review remains under pathologist control, molecular testing remains mandatory, and the score is framed as a triage aid for sequencing capacity.

The interpretive leap comes in the discussion section. The authors argue that above-chance slide-level performance is evidence that the model has learned mutation-related morphology from H&E alone. They point to smooth saliency overlays and to cross-site generalization as reasons to trust that conclusion. The packet contains no region-level molecular truth. Every training label is attached to an entire whole slide, with tumor regions within the same case often heterogeneous and broad subtype patterns able to correlate with genotype at the slide level. The memo acknowledges intratumoral heterogeneity as a biological reality and treats it as irrelevant because the operational decision is made per case, not per region.

That defense is partly true and partly too broad. A slide-level score may still be useful as a triage signal, and the slide-level metric does not by itself justify the stronger statement that the network has localized mutation-specific morphology rather than exploiting coarser subtype or case-level proxies. The board is therefore being asked to approve not only a workflow aid and also a scientific interpretation of what the model has learned. The prompt is written to make those two claims feel aligned, with only one of them directly evaluated. The launch packet includes a proposed sixty-day silent phase, weekly concordance review with thoracic pathology, and a later request to cite the model as biological evidence in grant renewal materials.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The slide-level model may be acceptable as a case-level triage aid, but slide-level AUROC is mismatched to the stronger claim that the network has learned region-level mutation-specific morphology.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate the frozen model on region-level or microdissected tumor areas with localized molecular ground truth, or show that performance persists in subtype-restricted analyses that remove obvious case-level proxies.

planted_issues.description: Whole-slide AUROC measures case ranking and does not establish that the learned signal corresponds to localized mutation-specific morphology within tumor regions.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding the mismatch between slide-level labels and region-level biological interpretation in computational pathology.

planted_issues.description: Above-chance whole-slide performance can arise from genotype-correlated histologic subtypes or other case-level proxies rather than direct mutation morphology.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires domain knowledge that histologic subtype correlations can support slide-level prediction without validating a finer biological mechanism.

planted_issues.description: Generalizing across hospitals reduces some confounds but does not prove that the learned signal is the intended biological one rather than a transferable proxy.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires recognizing that external whole-slide success is not the same as mechanistic validity.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should separate the operational case-level triage claim from the scientific claim about mutation-specific morphology and note that only the first is directly evaluated.
The critique should ask for region-level or subtype-restricted testing rather than dismissing the whole-slide model outright.
ideal_defense: The defense can reasonably say that the immediate workflow use is case-level prioritization, so whole-slide AUROC is relevant evidence for that narrow task.
But the defense should concede that the memo overinterprets the result when it claims the model has demonstrated localized mutation morphology from H&E alone.
