ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Region-level or subtype-restricted evaluation collapses the signal toward chance, indicating the slide-level result was carried by coarser case proxies.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The model retains meaningful performance on localized or subtype-restricted evaluation, supporting the stronger biological interpretation.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model remains useful for case-level triage while the mechanistic interpretation remains unresolved.

ground_truth_rationale: The prompt’s tension is between a defensible operational claim and an overbroad mechanistic one. Slide-level labels and metrics can support case-level triage, but they do not establish what the model is looking at inside the slide. That makes the calibrated answer mixed rather than purely critical.
difficulty_justification: This is hard because the memo includes many good practices—patient-level separation, multi-site data, stain normalization—that neutralize the obvious confound checklist. The remaining flaw is subtler: the metric supports one claim but not the more ambitious one. Single-pass reviewers often fail DRQ and ETD by either attacking the entire project or accepting the biology story without asking for localized validation.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failures are DRQ, ETD, and IDP because the case is designed so that a narrow defense is real while the stronger interpretation is not.

source_paper: Omixer: multivariate and reproducible sample randomization to proactively counter batch effects in omics studies
task_prompt:
BIOMARKER DISCOVERY COMMITTEE MEMO — SERUM METABOLITE PANEL. The translational omics group is asking for approval to advance a serum metabolite classifier into analytical verification after a retrospective case-control study identified a compact disease signature with apparently strong discrimination. The discovery set includes several hundred samples processed over multiple plates, and the memo stresses that the laboratory followed ordinary good practice: a random plate layout was generated before measurement, pooled quality controls were inserted throughout the run order, and a standard batch-correction method was applied during preprocessing to remove any residual plate effects. The resulting model achieved a high internal AUC, and the panel is now being described as one of the most reproducible signatures the group has produced in years.

The reassuring tone partly comes from the way the randomization story is told. The study had to accommodate paired relatives, masked control wells, and a few late-arriving specimens, so the final plate map was assembled from one feasible random draw rather than from a large search over candidate layouts. The memo treats that as entirely sufficient because randomization, by definition, should break systematic associations. A short table reports no alarming imbalances in age or sex by plate. Disease status is slightly more concentrated on a subset of early plates because those samples were physically available first. The authors dismiss this as a minor nuisance that would be soaked up by post-hoc correction. They point out that PCA after correction no longer shows a dramatic plate axis, and they present this as evidence that the remaining signal is biological.

Operationally, the study looks like a familiar translational program. Samples moved from accessioning into aliquoting, freezer pull, extraction, and mass-spectrometry acquisition under the regular biobank chain-of-custody process. Pooled quality controls were injected at planned intervals, the assay team tracked internal standards in the same run dashboard used for prior projects, and the biomarker committee received a plate-by-plate summary in its monthly review meeting. The resulting classifier is being used for a downstream go or no-go decision about assay verification, not just for exploratory ranking. Program leadership likes that the candidate panel is compact enough to migrate onto a more targeted verification assay if the committee gives approval this quarter.

What is not discussed at length is that if disease status and plate assignment remain meaningfully entangled, a corrected model can still carry technical structure into the signature, especially when the original layout was just one acceptable random draw among many. The memo instead treats the absence of a large obvious PCA split as practical proof that randomization succeeded. It also argues that because the classifier uses many metabolites rather than a single feature, any one residual plate artifact is unlikely to dominate. Wet-lab reviewers find this reassuring because it sounds like ordinary, disciplined metabolomics work rather than a fragile one-off analysis.

The document reads like a common wet-lab success story: reasonable randomization, familiar correction software, visually improved PCA, and a promising multivariate panel. None of those details is inherently suspicious. The hard question is whether the study design actually disentangled biology from plate assignment enough to justify advancing the signature, or whether a seemingly ordinary and suboptimal layout let phenotype and processing batch drift together in a way the memo underestimates. The requested decision is immediate advancement into analytical verification planning, vendor quotes for a targeted panel, and reservation of follow-on budget before the next portfolio review.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The disease signature is confounded by processing layout because phenotype and plate assignment were not proactively balanced in a multivariate way, and post-hoc correction plus prettier PCA does not establish that the classifier is biological rather than partially technical.

planted_issues.description: Using one acceptable random draw rather than optimizing balance across all key variables left disease status unevenly distributed across plates.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that one ad hoc random layout can still yield meaningful phenotype-batch correlation, especially in constrained omics studies.

planted_issues.description: Applying a standard correction method after measurement does not guarantee recovery of true biology when phenotype and batch were partially entangled at design time.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that batch correction cannot identify biology perfectly when technical and biological factors are correlated.

planted_issues.description: Visually improved PCA after correction is not sufficient evidence that a downstream predictive model is free of residual technical signal.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that low-dimensional batch plots can look improved while predictive leakage from technical structure remains.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should attack the design-and-claim chain: imperfect layout balancing created partial phenotype-plate correlation, and the memo then overrelied on post-hoc correction and PCA cosmetics.
The critique should not confuse this with a generic complaint that any multi-plate study is invalid; the problem is inadequate proactive balancing for the intended go/no-go use.
ideal_defense: The defense can say the current result may still be useful as exploratory prioritization for future validation chemistry.

But the defense should concede that the memo overstates reproducibility and cannot justify analytical verification without showing that the panel survives a better-balanced rerun or a layout-robust analysis.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A rerun with multivariate plate balancing or reference-aware analysis materially weakens or destabilizes the panel, showing that the original signature depended on layout structure.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The same panel remains strong under a better-balanced rerun and reference-aware analysis, indicating the original signal was mostly biological.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Some metabolites survive but the multivariate score shifts enough that the current go/no-go recommendation becomes premature.

ground_truth_rationale: The flaw is a hidden design confound rather than a visible contradiction. The memo relies on ordinary-sounding randomization and cleaner PCA after correction, but those are not enough when disease status was not well balanced across plates and the result is being used for a consequential advancement decision.
difficulty_justification: This is hard because every surface feature sounds reasonable and common in wet-lab omics. The reviewer has to know that a single random draw can still be poor, that PCA cleanup is not a proof of deconfounding, and that post-hoc correction is limited when phenotype and plate are correlated. Likely failed dimensions are IDR, DC, and IDP.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failures are IDR, DC, and IDP because many reviewers will either overtrust the correction workflow or default to broad anti-batch rhetoric without naming the specific design failure.

source_paper: Omixer: multivariate and reproducible sample randomization to proactively counter batch effects in omics studies
task_prompt:
MULTI-OMICS OPERATIONS REVIEW — FAMILY COHORT PLATE DESIGN. The population health biobank is defending a metabolomics analysis plan that some reviewers called 'too engineered' after seeing the sample-allocation spreadsheet. The project measures twins, parents, and unrelated controls on a constrained set of assay plates with fixed control wells and several specimen-pairing rules. Instead of drawing one simple random layout, the analysts generated many feasible candidate layouts under a recorded seed schedule, scored them on the joint correlation between biological variables and batch assignments, and selected the layout with the lowest overall imbalance and preserved the laboratory masks and paired-sample constraints. Concurrent pooled references are placed on every plate and the code used to generate the layout is part of the study record.

The operations note explains why the design became more structured than a simple random draw. The cohort includes twins, parents, and unrelated controls arriving from different freezer boxes and visit windows, and the laboratory has fixed control wells plus shipping constraints that limit how many related specimens can move together. A naive randomization could satisfy the surface rules and still align family structure, sex, case status, or visit timing with plate assignment in awkward ways. The analysts therefore generated many feasible candidate layouts under a recorded seed schedule and scored each candidate on the joint correlation between biological variables and batch assignments before choosing the layout with the lowest overall imbalance. The final map was loaded into the LIMS before any measurement and locked in the same study record that contains the masking rules.

The criticism arises because this plate map does not look naturally random to a casual reviewer. Co-twins are not always on the same plate, some plates contain intentionally heterogeneous mixes of sex and case status, and the final layout was chosen rather than accepted from the first valid randomization. A pattern-matching critic could say the team searched until the data looked favorable. The memo addresses that worry directly. The optimization happened before measurement, used only design variables available at that time, and minimized the risk that plate, box, family structure, or sex would align accidentally. The team further narrowed the scientific claim: the analysis is intended to support within-study association estimates and reference-aware model development, not universal absolute quantification across all labs everywhere.

Operationally, the workflow is conservative. Concurrent pooled references are placed on every plate, the laboratory team still plans to inspect post-run QC, compare reference trajectories across plates, and halt downstream modeling if an unanticipated technical drift appears. The bioinformatics team will also archive the candidate-layout scores, the selected seed, and the final plate manifest so that reviewers can reconstruct the pre-measurement design logic. The memo does not claim that careful layout eliminates every batch effect. It claims that careful layout is preferable to a single unoptimized random draw when multiple biological variables and logistical constraints must be balanced simultaneously. That position sounds unusual only because many review boards are used to equating truly random with methodologically pure, even when a naive randomization can accidentally align phenotype with plate.

The board is being asked whether this design should count as methodologically suspect because it is more structured than a coin flip, or methodologically stronger because it proactively reduces exactly the correlations that later undermine omics findings. The packet includes enough detail for the latter view to be defended if one reads it carefully. Approval this month would let the biobank release the final sample manifest to the assay vendor, retain the current freezer-pull schedule, and begin measurement with the recorded design intact.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The study design is methodologically sound for its stated scope because proactive multivariate layout optimization before measurement is an appropriate way to reduce batch confounding under real laboratory constraints.

planted_issues.description: A critic may wrongly treat pre-measurement layout optimization as outcome peeking even though it used only design metadata and was intended to reduce batch confounding.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding the difference between optimizing plate balance before measurement and tuning a model after seeing outcomes.

ideal_critique: A calibrated critique should limit itself to transparency questions, such as whether the candidate-layout search rules and seed schedule are fully archived and whether post-run QC stop rules are explicit.
It should not claim that selecting the best pre-measurement layout is equivalent to outcome-based p-hacking.
ideal_defense: The defense should emphasize that the search was conducted before measurement, using only known design variables, and that the resulting layout reduces exactly the phenotype-batch correlations that otherwise plague omics studies.
The defense can also note that the claim is properly narrow: balanced within-study analysis with reference-aware modeling, not universal batch immunity.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Audit records show the layout was changed after seeing assay results or that the stated search criteria depended on outcome information unavailable at design time.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Records confirm that layout optimization was fully pre-measurement, metadata-only, and documented, with the claimed narrow scope retained.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The design is valid but some documentation around the search archive or QC stop rules needs tightening before publication.

suspicious_but_valid_features: A chosen rather than first-drawn plate layout, heterogeneous composition within plates, and explicit candidate-layout scoring may look like manipulation to a casual reviewer, but here they are justified because all choices were made before measurement using only design metadata.
ground_truth_rationale: This is a defense-wins case because the study does exactly what a careful omics design should do under constraints: proactively reduce phenotype-batch correlation before the assay is run. The suspicious feature is the optimization step itself, but that is valid here because it is not informed by outcomes.
difficulty_justification: The case is hard because many reviewers reflexively equate 'more structured than naive randomization' with bias. A single-pass critic is likely to raise a domain-plausible false positive about design manipulation and fail IDP. The proper exoneration requires distinguishing layout optimization from post-outcome model tuning.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failed dimensions are IDP and DC because the surface pattern resembles p-hacking even though the chronology and scope make the design appropriate.

source_paper: Correcting batch effects in large-scale multiomics studies using a reference-material-based ratio method
task_prompt:
PROTEOMICS TRANSLATION REVIEW — SEPSIS TRIAGE SIGNATURE. The clinical proteomics team recommends advancing a blood-based classifier intended to distinguish early sepsis from sterile post-operative inflammation. The memo’s most persuasive figure is a PCA plot after batch correction: samples no longer separate cleanly by originating laboratory, and the corrected dataset supports a cross-validated classifier with apparently excellent discrimination. The authors describe this as strong evidence that modern harmonization can rescue unavoidable real-world collection differences and allow the hospital network to use a single model across sites.

The operational backstory seems ordinary. The first hospital shipped sepsis-enriched samples early in the study when assay capacity was concentrated in Lab A. Sterile inflammatory controls were added later through a second hospital whose specimens were profiled in Lab B after the platform contract expanded. Because the goal was to move quickly, no common reference material was run concurrently across both labs during the original measurement period. The team instead relied on a familiar empirical correction method during analysis and confirmed that the corrected data clustered more by phenotype than by lab. That visual success is treated as proof that the downstream classifier is now reflecting biology rather than site.

The packet includes enough process detail to feel reassuring. Sample accessioning, plasma isolation, freezer transfer, and shipment logs were documented through the regular clinical proteomics chain of custody. Both labs used the same broad platform family and standard operating procedures, the bioinformatics group locked its preprocessing code before the final modeling pass, and the translational review committee received side-by-side PCA plots in the monthly analytics forum. The classifier is internally cross-validated, the preprocessing is documented, and the memo openly acknowledges that lab and disease status were originally entangled. The authors simply believe the correction step solved that entanglement. They argue that if corrected PCA no longer shows a dominant lab axis and if the predictive model remains strong after correction, then the signal must be real.

A short section on limitations says future studies may add shared reference pools, and the current evidence is already sufficient for assay transfer planning. That reasoning is attractive because it turns a flawed acquisition history into a fixable analytics problem. It also supports concrete next steps. The clinical proteomics team wants approval to begin assay-bridging discussions with a vendor, define the triage reporting format, and reserve budget for a network pilot in the next fiscal quarter. Surgeons like the idea because sterile post-operative inflammation remains a frequent source of diagnostic uncertainty in the current pathway.

The central question is whether batch correction can legitimately support the belief that the signature has survived deconfounding under this collection history. If sepsis cases and controls were measured in different labs with no concurrent references, the correction algorithm has no external anchor separating biological differences from lab-specific shifts. The memo asks the review board to accept corrected internal structure as if it identified the biology. The launch recommendation is not merely exploratory; it includes assay-bridging work and vendor discussions based on the belief that the signature has survived deconfounding. The central question is therefore whether batch correction can legitimately support that belief under complete phenotype-batch confounding, or whether the apparent rescue is mathematically underidentified no matter how good the post-correction plots look.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Because disease status and laboratory are fully confounded and no concurrent reference materials anchor the measurements, the correction step cannot identify whether the resulting classifier reflects biology or lab effects, so the rescue claim is invalid.

planted_issues.description: Sepsis status and originating lab move together, so the study lacks the design information needed to distinguish biological from laboratory effects.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding identifiability limits in batch correction when batch and phenotype are completely confounded.

planted_issues.description: Without common reference materials measured across both labs, there is no external anchor to separate absolute laboratory shifts from true disease differences.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing why reference materials or balanced cross-batch design matter for cross-lab omics integration.

planted_issues.description: Cleaner PCA and strong internal cross-validation after correction do not establish that the recovered classifier is biological when the original confounding was unidentifiable.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires understanding that internal structure after harmonization can look plausible even when biology and batch remain inseparable.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should name the identifiability problem directly: once phenotype and lab are fully confounded, correction software has no ground truth to know what to preserve.
The critique should also resist the temptation to treat PCA improvement as decisive evidence; in this setting, prettier plots do not solve the missing design information.
ideal_defense: The defense can say the current analysis may still help generate hypotheses for a properly designed follow-up with concurrent references or balanced measurement.
But it should concede that the present memo cannot support assay-transfer or rescue claims as though deconfounding had been achieved.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A follow-up balanced cross-lab study with shared references fails to reproduce the same signature or shows strong lab sensitivity in the original score.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A balanced reference-anchored replication recovers nearly the same signature, showing the original corrected result happened to align with true biology.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Only a subset of proteins replicate, implying the original corrected model mixed true biology with lab-specific structure.

ground_truth_rationale: The key flaw is not bad software choice but missing identifiability: complete phenotype-lab confounding without shared references means the correction problem is underdetermined. Internal plots and internal CV can still look convincing, which is what makes the case hard.
difficulty_justification: This is hard because everything after correction looks tidy and many reviewers over-trust harmonization tools once PCA improves. The real flaw is a world-to-method mismatch, not an internal contradiction. Single-pass reviewers are likely to fail IDR, DC, and IDP by focusing on the wrong downstream metric rather than the missing cross-lab anchor.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failures are IDR, DC, and FVC because reviewers must recognize an identifiability problem that no amount of internal validation can fix.

source_paper: Multi-omics data integration using ratio-based quantitative profiling with Quartet reference materials
task_prompt:
NETWORK BIOBANK METHODS REVIEW — RATIO-BASED MULTI-OMICS INTEGRATION. The precision-health consortium is defending a cross-laboratory multi-omics integration workflow that some statisticians initially flagged as suspicious because raw values differ noticeably by site and platform. The project combines transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic measurements from matched biospecimens processed over many months. Rather than claiming that raw intensities are directly comparable, the team profiles a common reference material alongside study samples in every batch and transforms each study measurement into a ratio relative to the concurrent reference before downstream modeling. The integrated predictor is then trained on those ratio-based quantities.

The operational setup is broad and carefully documented. Participating labs send transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic outputs into the consortium data backbone after local QC, and each site runs the same reference material beside study samples in every batch. The common reference kits are tracked through the same logistics system as study specimens, reference acceptance criteria are reviewed at weekly platform meetings, and the transformation into ratios happens inside the shared analysis container before any cross-omics integration. The memo emphasizes that this design was chosen prospectively for a prospective consortium. The goal is to create comparable relative measurements across batches, labs, and omics types in the live network that actually produced the data.

To a casual reviewer, the workflow can look less rigorous than standard absolute quantification because it abandons the fantasy of one universal raw scale. Raw values from different labs still sit far apart if plotted directly, and the memo openly shows that. Critics therefore worry that the model is being rescued by arithmetic rather than by real reproducibility. The authors respond that this is exactly the point: in large multi-omics programs, absolute feature values drift for many technical reasons, and pretending otherwise creates the irreproducibility problem. The role of the common reference is to convert those drifting absolute measurements into comparable relative ones measured against the same anchor in each batch.

The memo is careful not to promise miracles. It does not claim that ratio profiling solves every hidden artifact or that the resulting model is transportable to historical archives where no concurrent references were run. It makes the narrower claim that within a prospective consortium that profiles the same common references concurrently, ratio-based quantities are the appropriate basis for integration across batches, labs, and omics types. The project also retains ordinary QC gates and does not suppress raw-data inspection; it simply does not mistake raw agreement for the criterion that matters most. Program operations would continue to monitor reference drift, flag failed batches, and reissue transformed matrices only after platform sign-off.

Reviewers who dislike the approach keep returning to the visible raw-value disagreement as if that alone invalidates the model. The governance board has to decide whether those raw discrepancies are proof of failure or an expected property of multi-site omics that the ratio design was built to address. Because the packet includes both the unflattering raw plots and the design logic for the reference-based transformation, the case is easy to misread if one treats absolute agreement as the only honest standard. Approval would affirm the consortium’s current integration plan, preserve the shared reference procurement schedule, and allow the next quarterly multi-omics release to proceed on the ratio-based workflow already embedded in the production analysis pipeline.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The work is methodologically sound for its stated prospective consortium scope because concurrent common references make ratio-based profiling a valid way to integrate multi-omics data despite raw cross-lab disagreement.

planted_issues.description: A critic may wrongly treat visible cross-lab differences in raw absolute values as proof of invalidity even though the workflow is explicitly based on reference-anchored relative quantities.
planted_issues.requires_external_knowledge: Requires knowing that in large multi-omics studies the correct integration target can be ratio-scale comparability rather than raw absolute agreement.

ideal_critique: A calibrated critique should ask only whether the common reference materials were truly concurrent and stable in every batch and whether the scope is kept prospective rather than retrofitted to archival data.
It should not claim that raw cross-lab disagreement alone invalidates a ratio-based design.
ideal_defense: The defense should emphasize that the claim is narrow and prospective: concurrent references convert drifting absolute scales into comparable relative measurements suitable for integration.
The defense can also concede that the same argument would not automatically apply to historical datasets lacking those references.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Audit records show that references were not run concurrently or that batches lacking references were silently included in the integrated model.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: All integrated batches include the common reference material concurrently and the model claim remains limited to that anchored prospective setting.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The ratio design is valid overall but some sub-cohorts lack fully concurrent references and require exclusion or narrower reporting.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Raw values disagree across labs, the analysis relies on relative ratios rather than absolute quantities, and the claim is intentionally limited to batches with concurrent references; these features can look evasive but are justified for the stated prospective integration problem.
ground_truth_rationale: This defense-wins case is about a methodology that looks suspicious if one expects absolute raw agreement across labs. The prompt provides the key justification in the design itself: the study measures common references concurrently and makes only a narrow prospective claim based on ratio-scale comparability.
difficulty_justification: The obvious finding—raw values differ by site—is a deliberate red herring. Reviewers who stop there will generate a false positive and fail IDP. Correct exoneration requires domain knowledge about reference-anchored relative quantification and the discipline to respect the memo’s narrow scope.
notes: Passed all 6 self-evaluation tests. Rubric self-test: likely failures are IDP and DC because a single-pass critic may equate raw disagreement with invalidity and miss that the model is built on concurrent ratio anchoring by design.
